summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 15 Pro lineup, identifying four key reasons to upgrade from the 14 Pro: titanium construction, camera improvements, the Action button, and USB-C. He's most enthusiastic about titanium, calling it an upgrade in every way that ends the 'national nightmare of too-heavy iPhones.' The camera system's new 24MP Photonic Engine and automatic Portrait mode represent meaningful computational photography advances. He pushes back firmly against the consensus that Apple switched to USB-C only because the EU forced them, arguing that MFi licensing revenue is negligible and Apple would never deliberately make the iPhone worse for a few dollars. The review is notably practical, drawing on his own photo library statistics to assess which features matter most.
key_points: Titanium is the biggest upgrade — dramatically lighter, better balanced, better looking, and functionally stronger than stainless steel, making it the best-feeling iPhone since the iPhone 5/5S era
The new Photonic Engine computationally fuses 48MP detail frames with 12MP quad-pixel low-light frames to produce 24MP images — a genuine next-generation step in computational photography
Automatic Portrait mode that works retroactively from regular photo mode will dramatically increase Portrait photo usage without any downside
The 5× telephoto exclusive to the Pro Max creates a real dilemma for users who prefer smaller phones but want the best camera system
Apple's opposition to the EU USB-C mandate isn't about Lightning loyalty — it's about being locked into USB-C in perpetuity when a superior successor (likely invented by Apple) eventually arrives
money_quotes: Our long national nightmare of too-heavy iPhones is over.
We shy away from admitting it, at least of late, but gadget lust is real. People are drawn to exquisite objects and premium materials.
The finish on the titanium frames of the iPhones 15 Pro is just terrific. It's elegant, not industrial, but it conveys a certain let's get to work seriousness.
Titanium is better functionally — it's stronger — and just as importantly, it's just fucking cool.
Apple is fundamentally a computer company, but materials engineering is an essential arm of the company.

And but we're supposed to believe that Apple deliberately made the iPhone's charging and data port worse, for years, for a fistful or two of dollars? My god, the sheer arrogance it takes to believe that Apple takes for granted an iota of the iPhone's success and enduring popularity.
Apple loves all its products, but it loves the iPhone most.
There will be a successor to USB-C eventually. It'll probably be invented by Apple.
key_insight: The iPhone 15 Pro's titanium construction represents a rare upgrade that benefits every single user — whether caseless or not — making the phone a fundamentally better handheld object, while the camera and USB-C changes are meaningful but more situational.
